item.title: When the Heat Wave Hits, the Cloud Feels It Too: AI's Summer Energy Problem
item.body:
## Server farms have a season

Most people think of data centers as climate-controlled fortresses, indifferent to the weather outside. But as summer heat waves roll across the United States, the facilities powering everything from streaming video to AI chatbots are quietly facing one of their toughest stretches of the year — and the boom in artificial intelligence is making the squeeze sharper.

## Why hot weather is hard on computers

Data centers generate enormous amounts of heat, and removing that heat is a constant, expensive battle. When outdoor temperatures spike, the problem compounds in two ways:

1. **Cooling gets less efficient.** Many facilities rely on outside air or evaporative cooling. The hotter and more humid it is, the harder those systems have to work to hit the same target temperature.
2. **The grid is under stress at the exact same moment.** Heat waves drive up residential and commercial air-conditioning demand, so data centers compete for power precisely when electricity is most strained and most expensive.

## AI raises the stakes

Training and running large AI models is dramatically more power-hungry than traditional web workloads. A single rack of modern AI accelerators can draw many times the power of a conventional server rack, concentrating heat into a smaller footprint. That density is forcing a rethink of infrastructure:

- **Liquid cooling** is moving from niche to mainstream, piping coolant directly to chips instead of relying on air.
- **Location strategy** increasingly favors cooler climates and regions with abundant renewable power.
- **Time-shifting workloads** — scheduling non-urgent training jobs for cooler nighttime hours — is becoming a real lever.

## What it means for the rest of us

The summer crunch is a preview of a structural challenge: AI demand is growing faster than clean power capacity in many regions. The companies that solve the heat-and-energy equation efficiently won't just save money — they'll set the pace for how fast AI can keep scaling. The next time your phone feels warm and your AI assistant answers instantly, remember there's a building somewhere fighting the same heat you are.
